coredata.dc:title: Gold nanostar@graphene quantum dot as a new colorimetric sensing platform for detection of cysteine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Abolghasemi-Fakhri, Zahra
Amjadi, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: We here report on a facile method for preparation of gold nanostar-graphene quantum dot (AuNS@GQD) composite, which produces highly active surfactant-free AuNSs. The etching reaction of this composite with Na2SO3 was studied and used as a new sensing strategy for colorimetric detection of nM levels of cysteine. In the presence of Na2SO3, the shape of AuNSs changes to sphere-like nanoparticles, leading to a distinct color change of solution from light green to indigo. This phenomenon results from the redox reaction of Au atoms at the apexes and sharp corners of the NSs with oxygen which leads to the formation of [Au(SO3)2]3−. Our studies indicated that the stars with larger sizes show greater activity in etching reaction since they have more branches and sharper tips. Due to the strong coordination between Au and thiols, pre-added cysteine can protect the AuNSs from SO3 2− etching and so the shape and the color of AuNSs remain unaltered. This anti-etching effect was used for the detection of cysteine with the detection limit as low as 0.35 nM. The developed colorimetric sensor was validated by HPLC method and applied for analysis of human plasma samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Gold nanostars
Anisotropic nanoparticles
Biothiols
Etching
Plasmatic sensor
Localized surface plasmon resonance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticle@graphene quantum dot (AuNP@GQD) seed solution
Synthesis of gold nanostar@graphene quantum dot (AuNS@GQD) composite
Etching reaction of AuNS@GQDs with Na2SO3

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of AuNS@GQDs (synthesized by using (A) 200 μL (B) 450 μL of AuNP@GQD seed solution) and their corresponding UV–Vis spectra (obtained for original synthesized solution).
(A) XRD pattern of AuNS@GQDs and GQDs, (B) Raman spectrum and (C) EDS spectrum of AuNS@GQD composite.
TEM images and corresponding absorption spectra of (A) AuNSs, (b) AuNSs in the presence of Na2SO3 and (c) AuNSs in the presence of Cys and Na2SO3 (color of the solutions is shown in inset).
Calibration curve and corresponding UV–Vis spectra for Cys at the optimum conditions. AuNS@GQD solution (62 ± 2 nm): 600 µL, phosphate buffer (0.20 M, pH 5.0): 25 µL, Na2SO3 (0.01 M): 200 µL.

Effect of interfering species on the Cys detection. The first column is the signal for Cys (50 nM) alone, and other columns are signals for Cys (50 nM) in the presence of interfering species (5.0 µM) (GSH 0.5 µM, Hcys 75 nM).
Results for the determination of cysteine in human plasma.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Mean of three determinations ± standard deviation.

head.ce:title: Gold nanostar@graphene quantum dot as a new colorimetric sensing platform for detection of cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Tabriz, Tabriz 5166616471, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: AuNS@GQD composites were prepared and its etching reaction with Na2SO3 has been used for detection of cysteine.

We here report on a facile method for preparation of gold nanostar-graphene quantum dot (AuNS@GQD) composite, which produces highly active surfactant-free AuNSs. The etching reaction of this composite with Na2SO3 was studied and used as a new sensing strategy for colorimetric detection of nM levels of cysteine. In the presence of Na2SO3, the shape of AuNSs changes to sphere-like nanoparticles, leading to a distinct color change of solution from light green to indigo. This phenomenon results from the redox reaction of Au atoms at the apexes and sharp corners of the NSs with oxygen which leads to the formation of [Au(SO3)2]3−. Our studies indicated that the stars with larger sizes show greater activity in etching reaction since they have more branches and sharper tips. Due to the strong coordination between Au and thiols, pre-added cysteine can protect the AuNSs from SO3 2− etching and so the shape and the color of AuNSs remain unaltered. This anti-etching effect was used for the detection of cysteine with the detection limit as low as 0.35 nM. The developed colorimetric sensor was validated by HPLC method and applied for analysis of human plasma samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Noble metal nanoparticles (NPs) are widely used as colorimetric sensing platforms because of their unique optical properties and high extinction coefficient. Most of such sensors are based on the aggregation of NPs [1], which have several severe drawbacks including instability, poor accuracy, and low selectivity. Toovercome these problems, strategies based on morphological change of NPs have recently been developed. The commonly used NPs in this strategy, are anisotropic Au or Ag NPs [2,3]. Gold nanostars (AuNSs), are among anisotropic NPs which have recently received increasing attention in SERS [4], plasmonic sensing [5,6] and chemiluminescence [7,8] applications. They have outstanding features such as high degree of anisotropy, shape-dependent plasmonic properties, plasmon tunability into the near IR region and plenty of hot spots at the tips or sharp edges [9]. AuNSs consist of a quasi-spherical core and several branches with sharp apexes. The localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) spectrum of AuNSs exhibits two typical peaks in the visible and NIR region, originating from the branch tips and inner core, respectively and is strongly sensitive to the particle size and degree of branching [7,8]. Unlike other AuNPs, the sharp tips of NSs provide a high electromagnetic field as well as higher sensitivity toward local changes in the dielectric environment that make them a suitable candidate for optical sensing applications [10]. Besides, AuNSs exhibit high activity at the sharp edges and tips, where the gold atoms can be easily etched by other molecules [6,11]. Etching may lead to a change in the color of the AuNS dispersion and a wavelength shift or intensity change of UV–Vis absorption that allows the detection of various analytes by these NPs [5,6,11–13].

One major problem with AuNSs is their synthesis procedure. A routine method for this purpose involves the use of poly(vinylpyrrolidone) (PVP) as a capping agent, which may lead to low sensitivity in colorimetric sensing applications. Therefore, attempts have been made for development of surfactant-free procedures for preparation of AuNSs [5,14]. Graphene-based particles such as graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) have been utilized as the modification agents or supports to increase the sensing performance of AuNSs [4]. Here we report on, for the first time, the preparation of AuNS-graphene quantum dots (GQDs) composite through a fast and straightforward two-step reduction method and its application as a novel colorimetric sensing agent. GQDs are graphene sheets with a size typically less than 20 nm [15]. Apart from excellent fluorescence, GQDs have many interesting physicochemical properties like dispersibility in water, functionalization according to their desired application, biocompatibility, chemical inertness and low cytotoxicity [16]. Such properties make them very suitable for fabricating GQDs/metal (e.g., Au and Ag) NPs hybrids with high potentials in (bio)sensing, catalysis and bioimaging [17]. The role of GQDs in our synthesis is preserving the NSs from aggregation and creating a high branching in AuNSs that leads to an increase in their activity in the subsequent reaction. Up to now, a few colorimetric probes based on GQD-metal nanocomposites have been developed for the assay of L-cysteine (Cys). Hai and co-workers [18] introduced a colorimetric method based on the r-GQDs coated AuNPs core–shell structure for the detection of Cys with a linear range of 10–100 nM. Chaicham et al. [19] proposed a colorimetric detection of Cys with a detection limit of 5.88 μM by utilizing GQDs/AuNPs nanocomposite. Detection mechanism in these methods is based on the aggregation of NPs, which suffer from the disadvantages discussed above. Recently, colorimetric assays based on the anti-etching of anisotropic nanoparticles such as gold nanorods [20] and silver nanoprisms [2] have also been used to detect Cys in biological samples. In the present work, cysteine is detected based on its anti-etching effect on AuNS@GQD–Na2SO3 reaction. In the absence of Cys, SO3 2− can effectively etch the apexes and sharp branches of AuNSs, leading to a definite color change from light green to indigo. On the contrary, in the presence of Cys, the formation of strong coordination between Au and thiol prevents the AuNSs from SO3 2− attack and the green color remains unmodified. AuNS@GQD is a novel material for etching reaction which can be applied as a platform for developing various sensors. As a proof-of-principle we showed that Cys can be detected by this platform with much lower detection limits than other anti-etching based methods.

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired with a Cary 100 spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained from a Siemens D 500 instrument (Germany). TEM images of NPs were acquired using a Leo 906 transmission electron microscope (Germany). Raman spectra were recorded on a P50C0R10 Spectrometer (Iran, Teksan) with 532 nm excitation wavelength. Elemental composition of AuNSs@GQDs was determined using energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (France, MIRA III).

Hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (III) was obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA, USA). Trisodium citrate, HCl, Na2SO3, AgNO3, NaOH, citric acid and ascorbic acid (AA) were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). A stock standard solution of Cys (1.0 mM) was prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of L-Cys powder (Merck) in water. Working 1.0 µM solution was prepared by serial dilution of this stock. Deionized water, obtained from aqua MAX - Ultra 370 water purification system (Young Lin instrument Co., Korea), was utilized for all experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gold nanoparticle@graphene quantum dot (AuNP@GQD) seed solution
ce:section.ce:para: 100 μL of GQDs solution (prepared via pyrolyzing citric acid [21], which detailed in Electronic Supplementary Information, ESI) was injected into a 10 mL of 0.25 mM of HAuCl4 solution and the mixture was stirred for 25 min. Afterwards, the solution was heated to a boiling state and 500 μL of 0.10 M sodium citrate was added slowly to the boiling solution. When the solution color altered to red, the reaction was complete. The obtained AuNP@GQD seed solution was filtered and maintained at 4 °C for further application.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of gold nanostar@graphene quantum dot (AuNS@GQD) composite
ce:section.ce:para: AuNS@GQD nanocomposites were prepared by a seed-mediated growth method. Different volumes of AuNP@GQD seed solution (200 and 450 µL which yielded AuNSs@GQDs with sizes of about 62 and 40 nm, respectively) and 10 μL of 1.0 M HCl were added into the 10 mL of 0.25 mM HAuCl4 solution at room temperature under quick stirring. Then, 45 μL of AgNO3 solution (8.0 mM) and 100 μL of AA (0.05 M) were injected simultaneously to the above mixture. After 40 min, the color of solution turns from red to green representing the formation of AuNS@GQD nanocomposites.
The synthesis procedure for PVP-capped AuNSs is given in ESI.

First, 25 μL of phosphate buffer (0.20 M, pH 5.0) was added into 600 μL of AuNS@GQD solution with size of 62 ± 2 nm. Then a proper volume of 1.0 µM working cysteine solution or plasma sample was injected to the mixture. Subsequently, 200 μL of aqueous solution of Na2SO3 (0.011 M) was added and total volume was adjusted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. After incubating at room temperature for 25 min, the UV–vis spectrum of the solution was recorded.

Typically, 500 μL of plasma sample (obtained from Blood Transmission Center, Tabriz, Iran) was vigorously mixed with 500 μL of acetonitrile and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 45 min to precipitate proteins. The clear supernatant solution which contained Cys was then collected and diluted to 50 mL with deionized water. A 100-μL portion of this solution was taken for analysis according to the general procedure.

AuNS@GQD composite was prepared via a seed-mediated growth method. The prepared NSs were separated by centrifugation and re-dispersed in ultrapure water for further use. The size and shape of AuNSs were controlled via changing the amount of Au seed solution. The lesser amount of seed solution leads to larger AuNSs with longer and sharper branches. This is accompanied by a shift in the long-wavelength plasmon band in UV–Vis spectrum [4]. TEM images and absorption spectra of two different sizes of AuNSs, prepared by using 200 and 450 µL seed solution, are shown in Fig. 1 . Their overall diameters are 62 ± 2 and 40 ± 1 nm, respectively. As depicted in the TEM images, with increasing the size of AuNSs the length of branches and their numbers increases. Correspondingly, the maximum absorption wavelength of AuNSs red-shifts and becomes broader. It is also worthy to point out that absorption spectrum of AuNP@GQD seed solution, contrary to AuNS@GQDs, exhibits a single peak located at 525 nm (Fig. S1, ESI).

Fig. 2 A displays the XRD pattern of AuNS@GQDs. The AuNS@GQDs nanocomposite exhibited three diffraction peaks at 2θ range from 13 to 75°, which belong to the (111), (200) and (220) planes of Au [22]. A broad peak centered at around 2θ = 26° can be attributed to (002) crystallographic structure of GQDs [23]. Raman spectrum of the AuNS@GQDs (Fig. 2B) exhibited distinct D peak at 1356 cm−1 (sp3-hybridized) and G peak at 1608 cm−1 (sp2-hybridized), indicating that GQDs were successfully embedded into the composite [24]. The elemental composition of AuNS@GQDs was characterized by EDS spectrum and revealed that the nanocomposite was composed of Au, O and C (Fig. 2C). All these observations corroborate the successful fabrication of AuNS@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Etching reaction of AuNS@GQDs with Na2SO3
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the applicability of as-prepared nanocomposite as a colorimetric etching-based sensing platform, we explored its behavior in the reaction with Na2SO3. Upon addition of Na2SO3 to the colloidal solution of AuNS@GQD at pH 5, the tip plasmon peak of nanocomposite exhibits a considerable blue-shift. This is probably due to the fact that a redox reaction occurs between AuNS@GQD and oxygen in the presence of SO3 2− to form [Au(SO3)2]3− complex. The standard redox potential of [Au(SO3)2]3− /Au is reported to be −0.045 V [25] or 0.116 V [26] which is much lower than that of the O2/H2O (0.934 V calculated according to Nernst equation at pH 5 at room temperature), so oxygen can efficiently oxidize the Au atoms at the active sites of NSs and forms [Au(SO3)2]3− complexes in the solution. The overall redox reaction can be written as 2Au + 4SO3 2− + ½O2 + 2H+ = 2[Au(SO3)2]3− + H2O [25]. This etching reaction leads to the morphology transformation of NSs to sphere-like NPs, which, in turn, causes a color change of solution from light green to indigo. TEM images in Fig. 3 clearly confirm the shape change of NSs. We found that the larger NSs create greater blue-shift indicating their higher activity in etching reaction due to having more and sharper tips (Fig. S2, ESI). The preferential etching at the tips of AuNSs (as clearly seen in TEM image of Fig. 3B) is related to the fact that Au atoms at these positions have a fewer coordination number and higher chemical activity than atoms at other regions. For comparison, we used spherical AuNPs with sizes of 20 and 65 nm (synthesized according to the method reported by Bastús et al [27]) in the etching process, but no reaction was detected (Fig. S3, ESI). We also used conventional PVP-capped AuNSs with similar sizes in this reaction. As shown in Fig. S3 (ESI), these NSs do not exhibit any shift in UV–Vis spectra, which is due to the protective effect of PVP, leading to inactivity of Au atoms. All evidence shows the special activity of AuNS@GQDs in etching reaction with sulfite.

In the next step, we examined the possibility of detection of Cys based on its anti-etching effect on AuNS@GQD–Na2SO3 reaction. We found that when Cys and SO3 2− are added sequentially, sulfite ions are not able to etch the AuNSs due to the strong coordination between Au and thiols, which effectively inhibits the etching process and keeps the shape and the color of AuNSs unchanged. The TEM image of the mixture of AuNS@GQDs and SO3 2−, in the presence of Cys (Fig. 3C), confirms this result. Also, as shown in the corresponding UV–Vis spectrum, no change occurs in the position of the AuNSLSPRpeak in the presence of Cys. These results indicate that Cys can efficiently protect the AuNSs from SO3 2− etching and enhance the stability of Au atoms at the tips and corners. Based on this finding, we developed a sensitive colorimetric method for the detection of cysteine. The effect of experimental conditions including pH, concentrations of Na2SO3 and buffer, the volume of AuNS@GQDs and incubation time on the wavelength shift was studied and optimized. The results are given in Fig. S4 (ESI). Afterward, analytical performance of the sensor for determination Cys, was evaluated. Fig. 4 demonstrates the calibration curve together with the corresponding UV–Vis spectra and color changes for Cys. The sensor has a linear behavior in the 1.25 to 100 nM concentration range (R2 = 0.999) with a detection limit (3σ rule) of 0.35 nM. The repeatability in terms of relative standard deviation (RSD %) was 1.9% for five replicate determinations of 50 nM Cys. The selectivity of the sensor toward some major interfering species was examined. As can be seen in Fig. 5 common species found in plasma do not interfere with Cys detection at 100-fold concentration except glutathione (GSH) and homocysteine (Hcys). GSH does not interfere at 10-fold concentration, and besides, its normal concentration in plasma (<0.54 µM) is much lower than that of cysteine [28–30]. Hcys is quite similar to Cys and so has a similar behavior, but its concentration in plasma is also much lower than Cys and so will not interfere with it. To prove this, the HPLC chromatograms of plasma samples were obtained and compared to Hcys standard (Fig. S5, ESI). As can be seen, while a distinct peak appears for Cys, there is no peak for Hcys in these chromatograms indicating that its concentration is much lower than Cys. All these results prove the selectivity of the sensor for analysis of plasma samples for Cys.

Comparison of the analytical characteristics of this method with those of some recently reported methods (Table S1, ESI) indicates that the developed sensor has better limit of detection (LOD) than most other methods.

We further prepared AuNS@rGO composite (details of synthesis procedure are given in ESI) and compared its analytical performance with AuNS@GQD. The results showed that this composite has lower sensitivity for Cys detection; the calibration curve for Cys was linear in the range of 20.0–500 nM with the slope of 13.9 (Fig. S6, ESI). Besides, the synthesis of rGO is a cumbersome process and the resulting AuNS@rGO composites are less stable than AuNs@GQDs (3 days compared to 1 month).

The developed sensor was utilized for determination of Cys in human plasma samples. The results are summarized in Table 1 . An independent HPLC method [31] was also used for analysis of these samples to validate the method. As can be seen in Table 1, the obtained results were in good agreement with those obtained by our method. Moreover, spiking recoveries of 97–100% were obtained for Cys in the human plasma samples. These confirm the accuracy and reliability of the method for real-world analyses.

In conclusion, we prepared a composite of AuNSs and GQDs through a fast and simple two-step reduction method without using any surfactant. The integration of AuNSs into GQD protects the NSs from aggregation and increases their activity in etching process. We used sodium sulfite, for the first time, in the etching-based sensing strategy. In the presence of SO3 2−, the tip plasmon band of AuNSs remarkably blue-shifted due to the etching reaction and shape transformation. On the contrary, when Cys is pre-added to the solution, it interacts with NSs via Au–S bonding and prevents the reaction. Therefore the shape of AuNSs, and their LSPR band and color remained unmodified. This effect was employed to develop a highly sensitive and selective probe for detection of Cys in human plasma samples. It should be mentioned that AuNS@GQDs as a promising novel sensing platform can be applied to develop colorimetric sensors for other analytes of interest.
Zahra Abolghasemi-Fakhri: Investigation, Validation, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Mohammad Amjadi: Conceptualization, Supervision, Resources, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
